plan_free_base: Review the standard stochastic Schrodinger equation and Lindblad master equation for continuously measured quantum systems to establish baseline formalism
Identify the probability distribution over measurement record trajectories for a continuously monitored quantum system using the positive operator-valued measure framework
Express the conditional quantum state evolution as a stochastic map parameterized by the measurement record to prepare for path integral construction
Double the quantum state space by introducing a conjugate or auxiliary copy of the density matrix to form a canonical phase space analogous to classical Hamiltonian doubling
Identify canonical conjugate variables on the doubled state space by analogy with the classical momentum-coordinate pairing in the Martin-Siggia-Rose or Doi-Peliti formalisms

Construct the path integral representation of the joint probability for an initial state, a measurement record trajectory, and a final postselected state by slicing time into infinitesimal intervals and inserting resolutions of identity
Take the continuous-time limit of the discrete path integral to obtain a continuum path integral with a well-defined action functional over doubled state space trajectories
Identify the boundary terms in the action functional that encode the initial state preparation and the final postselection condition

Derive the equations of motion for the most probable trajectory by extremizing the action functional with respect to variations of both the state and conjugate variables, imposing boundary conditions at initial and final times

Show that in the limit of no postselection, the saddle-point equations reduce to the standard Lindblad master equation or stochastic Schrodinger equation, verifying the formalism recovers known results
Incorporate feedback control by introducing a Hamiltonian term in the action that depends on the measurement record history, and verify the action remains extremizable
Identify rare-event trajectories as instantons or saddle points of the action with postselection boundary conditions and characterize their probability via the on-shell action value
Compute Gaussian fluctuations around the saddle-point trajectory by expanding the action to second order and evaluating the resulting functional determinant

Compare the stochastic path integral formulation with Mensky restricted Feynman path integral approach to identify structural differences and clarify how the doubled state space generalizes earlier treatments

Summarize the complete formalism, listing the action functional, boundary conditions, saddle-point equations, and regime of validity, and outline applications to quantum state preparation and rare quantum events
plan_free_step: Identify the strategic goal: construct a stochastic path integral over a doubled quantum state space that encodes both forward evolution and postselection boundary conditions

Define the continuous measurement model: specify the stochastic master equation (Lindblad form) and the stochastic Schrödinger equation (SSE) driven by measurement readouts as the starting dynamical objects

Construct the discrete-time path measure by slicing the time interval into N steps and expressing the joint probability of a density matrix trajectory and measurement record as a product of conditional propagators
Double the state space by introducing an auxiliary (bra) density matrix lambda conjugate to rho, forming the canonical pair (rho, lambda) analogous to position-momentum in classical phase space
Derive the coherent-state or operator-basis resolution of identity over the doubled space to convert the discrete path measure into an integral over continuous field variables
Compute the log of the single-slice propagator to extract the incremental action dS, expressing it in terms of (rho, lambda, dy) and expanding to leading order in dt
Integrate out the measurement record dy to obtain the effective action S_eff[rho, lambda] governing rare trajectories, performing the Gaussian integral over the Wiener increments
Impose initial and final boundary conditions: pin rho(0) = rho_0 (state preparation) and enforce postselection rho(T) = rho_f via a boundary term or delta-function constraint in the path integral
Derive the Euler-Lagrange equations of motion by extremizing S_eff + S_bdy with respect to rho and lambda, obtaining Hamilton equations on the doubled quantum phase space

Verify recovery of standard limits: show that when postselection is absent (lambda = identity throughout), the saddle-point equation for rho reduces exactly to the unconditional Lindblad master equation
Identify the quantum Hamiltonian function H(rho, lambda) generating the canonical flow, and express the most-probable (optimal) measurement trajectory as the classical path of this Hamiltonian system
Incorporate feedback control by augmenting the Hamiltonian with a control term H_ctrl(rho, lambda, u(dy)) where u is a feedback policy; derive the optimal feedback law by further extremizing over u

Verify consistency with Mensky's restricted path integral: show that projecting the doubled-space action onto pure states and ignoring the lambda dynamics reproduces Mensky's restricted Feynman amplitude up to normalization

plan_free_base: Recognize that the original disk magnet (radius 2R, thickness d) produces field B0 at its center, which is on the axis of the disk at the midplane
Use the superposition principle: the full disk (radius 2R) equals the annular disk (inner radius R, outer radius 2R) plus the small disk (radius R), all with the same magnetization and thickness d
Identify that the center of the hollowed region coincides with the center of both the full disk and the removed small disk

Find the magnetic field at the center of a uniformly magnetized disk of radius r and thickness d on its axis at the midplane, using the known formula for axial field of a disk magnet expressed via surface current model or bound current model
Express B0 (field of full disk, radius 2R) in terms of M, d, and R using the formula from step 4
Express the field of the small removed disk (radius R) at its center using the same formula, with radius = R
Take the ratio of B_small to B0 to find B_small in terms of B0, R, and d by dividing the two expressions
Apply superposition to solve for B_annulus = B_full minus B_small, which gives B1 = B0 minus B_small
Write the explicit formula for the axial field at the center of a disk magnet: B = mu0 * M * [1 - d / sqrt(d^2 + 4r^2)] / ... using the standard result, then compute the ratio for r=R vs r=2R
Substitute the ratio back to express B1 as B0 multiplied by (1 minus the ratio), giving the final vector expression for B1 in terms of B0, R, and d
plan_free_step: Conceptually recognize that the original full disk (radius 2R) can be decomposed as the annular magnet plus the removed disk (radius R), both with the same uniform magnetization and thickness d.
Identify that B_0 is the field at the center of the full disk of radius 2R and thickness d, and express it as the sum of contributions from the annulus and the small disk of radius R.

Compute the magnetic field at the center of a uniformly magnetized disk of radius r and thickness d (with d << r) using the equivalent surface current or bound current model, expressing it in terms of magnetization M.
Apply the thin-disk on-axis field formula to compute B_small for a disk of radius R and thickness d, then scale to relate it to B_0 (which corresponds to radius 2R and same thickness d).
Use the thin-disk approximation result that on-axis center field scales as d/r to find B_small(R,d) in terms of B_0(2R,d): since field ~ d/(2r), halving r doubles the field, giving B_small = 2*B_0.

Solve for B_1 using B_1 = B_0 - B_small = B_0 - 2*B_0 = -B_0, then verify direction: the removed small disk would add field in the same direction as B_0 at its own center, so removing it subtracts that contribution, yielding B_1 = -B_0.

plan_free_base: Identify the effusion regime: since the hole is much smaller than the mean free path, molecules escape independently without collisions at the hole, so use the effusion (Knudsen) framework
Write the Maxwell-Boltzmann speed distribution for gas molecules in equilibrium at temperature T inside the container
Determine the effusion flux: the rate at which molecules with speed v escape through the hole is proportional to v times f(v), because faster molecules hit the hole more frequently
Identify the degrees of freedom for nitrogen (N2): 3 translational and 2 rotational (vibrational modes frozen out at typical temperatures), giving 5 total degrees of freedom

Compute the effusion-weighted average translational kinetic energy by evaluating the ratio of the integral of one-half m v-squared times v times f(v) over all speeds, divided by the integral of v times f(v) over all speeds

Evaluate the integrals using standard results: the integral of v-to-the-fifth times exponential of negative alpha v-squared equals one over alpha cubed, and the integral of v-cubed times exponential of negative alpha v-squared equals one over two alpha squared

Determine the average rotational energy per escaping molecule: since rotational motion is independent of translational speed, the effusion weighting does not bias rotational energy, so it equals the equilibrium average of k_B T (from 2 rotational degrees of freedom times one-half k_B T each)
Add translational and rotational contributions to obtain the total average energy per escaping nitrogen molecule
plan_free_step: Identify the effusion regime: since hole size is much smaller than mean free path, this is molecular effusion (Knudsen effusion), where molecules escape independently without collisions near the hole.

Write the flux-weighted speed distribution for effusing molecules: the rate at which molecules with speed v escape is proportional to v times the Maxwell-Boltzmann speed distribution f(v), giving a flux distribution phi(v) proportional to v * f(v).
Compute the normalization constant for phi(v) by integrating v^3 * exp(-mv^2/2kT) from 0 to infinity, using the standard integral result integral_0^inf v^3 exp(-alpha v^2) dv = 1/(2 alpha^2).

Determine the energy per escaping molecule: for diatomic nitrogen (N2), the total energy has translational kinetic energy (1/2)mv^2 plus internal (rotational/vibrational) degrees of freedom. At temperature T, the internal energy per molecule is fixed at (f_internal/2)kT, independent of speed, so average internal energy contribution is (5/2 - 3/2)kT = kT from rotational modes (2 rotational DOF at typical temperatures).

Compute the average translational kinetic energy of escaping molecules by evaluating <(1/2)mv^2> under phi(v): integrate (1/2)mv^2 * phi(v) dv, using integral_0^inf v^5 exp(-alpha v^2) dv = 1/alpha^3, yielding average translational KE = 2kT.

Sum translational and internal contributions to get total average energy per escaping molecule, then verify against known effusion energy result E_avg = (translational) + (rotational) = 2kT + kT = 3kT, and check dimensional consistency.

plan_free_base: Define the four free parameters of the likelihood scan: gamma (energy spectral index), ns (number of signal events), t0 (mean flare time in MJD), and dt (1-sigma Gaussian flare duration in days)
Run the maximum likelihood estimation on the real NGC 1068 dataset across the full four-dimensional parameter space to identify the best-fit values and best-fit TS
Perform a 2D likelihood scan over ns and dt while profiling over gamma and t0 at each grid point, recording the TS at each point in the ns-dt plane
Compute delta-TS at each grid point by subtracting the scanned TS from the best-fit TS
Identify the local minimum at small dt and the global minimum at maximum allowable dt in the 2D likelihood surface to characterize the structure of the likelihood space

Recognize that the global minimum at the boundary of the allowed dt range indicates the best-fit may be unphysical or at the edge of the physical parameter space, motivating use of Feldman-Cousins instead of standard Wilks contours
Generate a large ensemble of background-only pseudo-experiments by scrambling the dataset in right ascension to randomize the arrival directions of events while preserving detector response
For each background pseudo-experiment, run the full maximum likelihood scan over the four-dimensional parameter space and record the best-fit TS and best-fit parameter values

Generate a large ensemble of signal-injected pseudo-experiments by injecting simulated signal events at each grid point in the ns-dt plane (for fixed gamma and t0) on top of scrambled background datasets
For each signal-injected pseudo-experiment at each injected ns-dt grid point, run the full likelihood scan and record the best-fit TS and best-fit ns and dt values

For each grid point in the true ns-dt injection space, construct the Feldman-Cousins acceptance interval by determining which reconstructed ns-dt values fall within the desired confidence level ordering based on likelihood ratios relative to the best-fit

Invert the acceptance intervals to construct the Feldman-Cousins confidence region in the true ns-dt space by identifying which injected parameter values have acceptance intervals that include the observed best-fit values from the real data

Compare the Feldman-Cousins confidence region to the standard Wilks contours derived from the delta-TS map using four degrees of freedom to assess where the two methods agree or diverge, particularly near the boundaries of physical parameter space

Interpret the resulting confidence region in the context of the NGC 1068 analysis to assess whether the data favor a flaring hypothesis with a specific duration dt or are consistent with steady emission, noting where the physical boundary at large dt influences the interval
plan_free_step: Identify the statistical challenge: the 2D likelihood space in ns-dt has a local minimum at small dt and a global minimum at maximum allowable dt, suggesting the data is consistent with background and standard Wilks' Theorem contours may be unreliable or produce unphysical intervals

Run background-only scrambled trials (pseudo-experiments) by randomizing the dataset in right ascension to break any true signal correlation, generating a large ensemble of TS values under the null hypothesis
For each point in the 2D ns-dt parameter grid, compute the profile likelihood by maximizing over the nuisance parameters gamma and t0, producing a TS map over the scanned ns-dt space
Construct the delta-TS map by subtracting each scanned TS value from the best-fit TS (delta-TS = TS_best-fit - TS_truth) across the entire ns-dt grid

Inject signal pseudo-experiments at many discrete (ns_inj, dt_inj) grid points by adding simulated signal events drawn from the assumed Gaussian flare model on top of scrambled background, then re-running the full likelihood scan for each injection to obtain the distribution of best-fit TS and recovered parameters

For each injected signal point, compute the delta-TS threshold such that the observed delta-TS falls within the confidence belt at the desired confidence level (e.g., 90%), building the Feldman-Cousins ordering principle from the signal-injected trial distributions

Apply the Feldman-Cousins confidence belt to the observed data: for each (ns, dt) hypothesis, determine whether the observed delta-TS is within the acceptance region defined by the belt, thereby constructing the FC confidence interval in the 2D ns-dt space

Verify coverage of the FC intervals by checking that across the ensemble of background and signal trials, the true injected parameter values are contained within the constructed intervals at the stated confidence level

Interpret the resulting FC confidence region in the context of the NGC 1068 analysis: if the interval excludes ns=0 for some range of dt, this indicates evidence for a flare; if the interval includes ns=0 across all dt, set an upper limit on the flare signal strength and constrain whether the emission is steady or transient

plan_free_base: Write the susceptibility chi in terms of polarizability alpha and number density n using the relation chi equals n times alpha divided by epsilon zero
Apply Curie law by substituting alpha equals K divided by T into the susceptibility expression to get chi as a function of T
Express electric field E in terms of polarization P and temperature T using the relation P equals chi times epsilon zero times E, solved for E
Set up an infinitesimal Carnot cycle in the T-P plane with small changes dT and dP to derive a thermodynamic identity

Use the thermodynamic identity from the Carnot cycle to derive a relation between the partial derivative of entropy with respect to P at constant T and the partial derivative of E with respect to T at constant P
Compute the partial derivative of E with respect to T at constant P using the expression for E from step 3
Integrate the Maxwell relation over P to find entropy S as a function of T and P, identifying the temperature-dependent integration constant
Use the internal energy expression and thermodynamic relation to find f(P) by computing dU from the first law and comparing with U equals CT plus f(P)

plan_free_step: Express the electric susceptibility χ(T) using Curie law: since P = χ(T)ε₀E and each dipole has polarizability α = K/T, relate χ to n, α, and T to get χ(T) = nα/ε₀ = nK/(ε₀T)

Set up the infinitesimal Carnot cycle in (T, P) space: identify the four quasi-static legs (isothermal polarization at T, adiabatic depolarization to T-dT, isothermal depolarization at T-dT, adiabatic repolarization to T) and write the net work done and net heat absorbed
Apply the Carnot efficiency relation δW_net/δQ_hot = dT/T to the infinitesimal cycle to derive the Maxwell relation (∂E/∂T)_P = -(∂S/∂P)_T
Compute (∂E/∂T)_P by inverting P = χ(T)ε₀E to get E = P/(χ(T)ε₀) = PT/(nK), then differentiate with respect to T at constant P to get (∂E/∂T)_P = P/(nK)

Integrate the Maxwell relation to find S(T,P): (∂S/∂P)_T = -P/(nK), so S = S₀(T) - P²/(2nK), then use dU = TdS + EdP (at constant volume) to find f(P) by integrating (∂U/∂P)_T = E - T(∂S/∂P)_T·... apply (∂U/∂P)_T = E + T·P/(nK) - E... simplify using thermodynamic identity (∂U/∂P)_T = T(∂S/∂P)_T + E, giving (∂U/∂P)_T = -TP/(nK) + P/(χε₀) = -TP/(nK) + PT/(nK) = 0

Conclude that f(P) is independent of P (constant), so U(T,P) = CT up to an additive constant; verify consistency with the Curie law and the Carnot cycle result, confirming the internal energy depends only on temperature
